# CIP-30-DeepLink — full integration reference

> @fluxpointstudios/cip30-deeplink-client — the dApp-side reference SDK for CIP-30-DeepLink (CIP-186).
> Everything-in-one-fetch doc for AI agents and developers. Concise index: https://cip30-inspector.vercel.app/llms.txt

## What it is

CIP-30-DeepLink is a deep-link wire protocol that lets a native mobile dApp ask an installed Cardano
wallet to perform CIP-30 operations (connect, signTx, signData, address/network reads) over the OS's
URL-handling mechanism. No relay server, no embedded WebView, no QR, no WebSocket. The dApp navigates
to the wallet via a deep link; the wallet processes the request and redirects back to the dApp with an
encrypted, signed response in the query string.

Transport security: each payload is NaCl-box encrypted (X25519 + XSalsa20-Poly1305) and the wallet
signs every response with a session-binding Ed25519 key. The connect handshake is FAIL-CLOSED — the
SDK refuses to seat a session unless the response is signed, method-tagged, nonce-echoed, and verifies.

## Install

    npm install @fluxpointstudios/cip30-deeplink-client

One runtime dependency: tweetnacl. Runs in the browser and Node. Ships ESM + CJS + TypeScript types.

## The round-trip model (read this first)

A deep-link operation is NOT a single async call. `connect()` and `signTx()` navigate the page to the
wallet; the wallet redirects back to your `redirect` URL on the next page load. So:

1. On EVERY page load, call `await client.resume()` FIRST to recover any pending response.
2. Then call `connect()` / `signTx()` to start a flow (these navigate away).

The result of a flow always arrives via `resume()` on the return load — never as the return value of
`connect`/`signTx`. The SDK persists in-flight state (the dApp keypair, the request nonce, the pending
op) in `storage` (localStorage by default) across the navigation.

## Quickstart

```ts
import { DeepLinkClient } from "@fluxpointstudios/cip30-deeplink-client";

const client = new DeepLinkClient({
  wallet: "yuti",                 // deep-link scheme cip30dl-yuti
  chain: "cardano:preprod",       // or cardano:mainnet / cardano:preview
  resolveTxHash,                  // your tx builder's BLAKE2b-256 hasher (MeshJS/Lucid/CSL)
});

// 1. Recover any pending wallet response (run first, every load).
const resumed = await client.resume();
if (resumed?.kind === "connect") {
  const session = resumed.session;   // { addresses, signingPublicKey, chain, network, expiresAt, ... }
}
if (resumed?.kind === "signTx") {
  const { witnessSet, txHash, signatureValid } = resumed.result;
}

// 2. Start connect (navigates to the wallet; result returns via resume()).
await client.connect({ name: "MyDapp", url: "https://mydapp.example", iconUrl: "..." });

// 3. Once connected, request a signature.
await client.signTx({ tx: cborHex });
```

## API

### new DeepLinkClient(options)
- wallet: string — wallet id; sets the deep-link scheme `cip30dl-<wallet>`.
- chain: "cardano:mainnet" | "cardano:preprod" | "cardano:preview".
- redirectUrl?: string — where the wallet returns; defaults to the current page URL in a browser,
  required outside one. Must be an https universal link the wallet can bind to your host, or a custom
  scheme you registered.
- storage?: KeyValueStore — persists in-flight state across the redirect; defaults to localStorage,
  required outside a browser.
- resolveTxHash?: (txCbor: string) => string — your tx builder's BLAKE2b-256(tx_body) hasher, for
  signTx. Optional if you always pass `commit`.
- signFormat?: "sdk-legacy" | "spec" — response wire format. sdk-legacy (default): the response
  decrypts to raw transaction_witness_set CBOR (Yuti). spec: the CIP-0186 { commit, witnessSet, txHash }
  envelope; the dApp verifies the commit echo (mismatch => -2) (Gero). connect is byte-identical across both.
- httpsPrefix?: string — an https universal-link prefix the wallet advertises (e.g.
  https://cip30dl.wallet.io/cip30dl/v1/). When set, requests use the https form instead of the custom
  scheme. iOS Safari opens universal links reliably but drops custom-scheme navigations.

### connect(dappInfo): Promise<void>
dappInfo = { name, url, iconUrl? }. Navigates to the wallet. Session delivered by resume().

### signTx(opts): Promise<void>
opts = { tx, partialSign?, vkeyHints?, commit?, ttlSeconds? }. tx is unsigned CBOR (hex). Navigates to
the wallet. Witness delivered by resume(). Requires a prior connected session.

### resume(url?): Promise<ResumeResult>
Recover a pending response (defaults to location.href). Returns { kind: "connect", session },
{ kind: "signTx", result }, or null. THROWS DeepLinkRejection on a wallet rejection, or an Error on a
decryption/verification failure. A returned signTx witness is always signature-verified.

### getSession(): Session | null
The active session, or null if none / expired (an expired session is cleared automatically).

### disconnect(): void
Forget the session and dApp keypair.

### Low-level pure helpers (custom transports / server-side / testing)
buildConnectUrl, buildSignTxUrl, decodeConnectResponse, decodeSignTxResponse, canonicalSubject,
canonicalSubjectCandidates, b64uEncode, b64uDecode.

### Types
- Session = { session, network, addresses, chain, walletId, expiresAt, signingPublicKey, walletKey }.
- SignTxResult = { witnessSet, txHash, signatureValid }.
- DeepLinkRejection extends Error = { errorCode: number, walletMessage: string }.

## signTx specifics
- The wallet returns a WITNESS SET, not a signed tx. Splice it into the body with your serialization
  library, or POST { txCbor, witnessSet } to your backend to assemble + submit (keeps CSL/Mesh off the client).
- commit = BLAKE2b-256(tx_body) (the Cardano tx hash) binds the request to a specific tx. Wire
  resolveTxHash once, or pass commit (base64url of the 32-byte hash) per call.

## Verification semantics (what resume() enforces)

connect (first contact, fail-closed): the response MUST carry method=connect (domain separation), an
echo of the request nonce, and the wallet's Ed25519 signature over the canonical subject. The SDK
box-decrypts the session, verifies the signature against the signingPublicKey inside that payload, then
checks echo == the nonce it sent. Rejections: missing method/signature/echo or bad signature =>
errorCode -10 (ResponseSignatureInvalid); echo mismatch => -5 (NonceReplay).

signTx: every response is Ed25519-signed over the canonical subject, keyed by the signingPublicKey
pinned at connect. A returned witness is always signature-verified; a bad signature rejects.

Trust boundary (honest): connect is first contact, so the signature proves the response is consistent,
untampered, replay-bound, and pins the session key — it does NOT by itself prove WHICH wallet replied.
That rests on the OS routing the scheme to the paired wallet plus the in-wallet consent screen.

## Canonical subject (the one interop-critical algorithm)

The signed message is: "cip30dl-v1\n" + <scheme>://<host-lowercased>[:port]<path> + "?" + the query
params (excluding signature) sorted by key in lexicographic byte order, each key and value
strict-unreserved percent-encoded ([A-Za-z0-9._~-] literal, else %XX uppercase), joined by &. Default
ports (443/80) omitted; path byte-preserved.

Empty-path caveat: an authority-only redirect (https://host, no /) is '' under RFC-3986/Dart but '/'
under WHATWG URL.pathname — verifiers SHOULD accept both (the SDK's canonicalSubjectCandidates does).

Deterministic vs randomized Ed25519: a conforming verifier VERIFIES the signature; it does not
byte-compare it. Randomized/hedged signers (Apple CryptoKit) produce different-but-valid signature bytes
than deterministic ones (tweetnacl/libsodium). The known-answer vector's signature is a deterministic
reference; verify, don't byte-match.

## Conformance

A byte-exact known-answer vector (fixed keys => reproducible signature + response URL, plus the -5/-10
reject cases) and a cross-language conformance kit live in the repo under conformance/. There's a live
inspector to paste a response and watch every check go green/red.

## Wallet support

Fail-closed, so end-user signing needs a wallet that emits the signed connect: Gero (implemented +
verified end-to-end against this SDK), Yuti (wallet-side reference). Build and test your whole
integration without a wallet via the inspector + the bundled fake wallet (test/fake-wallet.ts).

## Links
- npm: https://www.npmjs.com/package/@fluxpointstudios/cip30-deeplink-client
- Source: https://github.com/Flux-Point-Studios/cip30-deeplink-client

- Inspector: https://cip30-inspector.vercel.app
- Conformance kit: https://github.com/Flux-Point-Studios/cip30-deeplink-client/tree/release/v0.1.0-public/conformance
- Yuti wallet (reference): https://github.com/Flux-Point-Studios/yuti
